title: How I'd Learn AI From Scratch in 2026 (skip the useless 80%)
summary: Jeff Su presents a prioritized learning strategy for AI in 2026 centered on picking one model to master, building practical context through real projects, and assembling AI systems with tools. The guidance dismisses rote prompting frameworks in favor of deeper model understanding and contextual work.
writeup: A structured three-level curriculum for getting practical value from AI tools, cutting through the noise of outdated tutorials. The through-line is a single shift: context quality beats prompt engineering, and the right architecture (projects, then AI systems) compounds that context over time. The talk moves from model selection through context strategy through persistent memory structures, grounding each step in concrete examples rather than frameworks.
key_points: Model capability convergence is the central reason to stop agonizing over which frontier model to use: the Artificial Analysis benchmarks show top models clustering tightly in the top-right performance quadrant, making the practical gap negligible for most users.

The recommended shortlist narrows to three options (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) based on access, work type, and preference: ChatGPT for research and web search, Claude for writing and coding, Gemini for mixed-media work and Google Workspace users.

Context beats prompt engineering: giving the AI real examples of approved outputs (past status updates, reference documents) consistently outperforms elaborate prompt frameworks, because examples carry implicit constraints a user would never explicitly articulate.

The OC framework (Outcome + Context) replaces memorizing prompting patterns: specify a clear goal and supply relevant reference material, and the model infers role, format, and tone more accurately than explicit instruction would.

Projects in ChatGPT and Claude (Gems in Gemini) solve the repetition problem for recurring work by storing persistent instructions, knowledge files, and auto-updated memory, so constraints set once apply to every conversation in that workstream.

AI systems extend what projects cannot do: they cross-reference multiple project silos and update their own rules after feedback, so learnings compound; the spectrum runs from Gemini Spark (minimal setup, less control) through Claude Cowork (more control, some setup) to Claude Code and OpenAI Codex (fully customizable, code-comfortable users).
